---
title: "A platelet has no nucleus and a lot to do"
description: "Anucleate, short-lived, and running sophisticated signalling. Why it's interesting to model."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [research, biology]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in.]*

A platelet is a cell fragment. It has no nucleus, no DNA to transcribe, a lifespan of about ten days, and yet a healthy adult carries ~10¹² of them in circulation, each running a surprisingly sophisticated signalling repertoire. They're the only cell in the body that has to do its job without being able to change its mind.

## What it is

*[Derived from megakaryocyte fragmentation; anucleate but contains pre-loaded mRNA, mitochondria, dense granules, alpha granules; size, count, lifespan.]*

## What it does

*[Haemostasis (the obvious one), but also: immune signalling, inflammation, wound healing, vascular integrity. Not just a clotting cell.]*

## What's strange about it

*[No transcription, so all proteins are either pre-loaded or translated from pre-loaded mRNA. Finite resources. Cannot adapt to chronic stimuli the way nucleated cells can. The molecular biology is "what you see is what you get."]*

## Why it's interesting to model

*[The closed system is a feature. A wcEcoli-style model has a tractable scope because there's no transcriptional regulation to worry about — the protein inventory is set at birth.]*

## Open questions I'm chasing

*[Specific to my dissertation — fill in as the work progresses.]*
